at
 MGs
 [2,6]. Other
 notable
 examples
 are,
 outbreaks
 of
 inﬂuenza
at
 a
 World
 Youth
 Day
 (WYD)
 [7], a
 Winter
 Olympiad
 [8]
 and
 a
Rock
 Festival
 [9],
 and
 historically
 of
 cholera
 at
 Kumbh
 Mela,
 one
of
 the
 largest
 gatherings
 in
 the
 world
 that
 involves
 ritual
 bathing
in
 the
 Ganges
 in
 India
 [10]. These
 and
 other
 health
 hazards
 asso-
ciated
 with
 MGs
 have
 been
 well
 reviewed
 [11–15]. It
 is
 likely
that
 a
 substantial
 proportion
 of
 these
 infections
 can
 be
 prevented
by
 immunisations.
 Vaccinations
 against
 meningococcal
 disease,
inﬂuenza,
 yellow
 fever
 and
 polio
 are
 recommended
 for
 attendees
at
 certain
 MGs
 [3,16]. However,
 the
 burden
 of
 vaccine
 preventable
diseases
 (VPDs)
 associated
 with
 MGs
 has
 not
 been
 quantiﬁed
through
 a
 formal
 review.
The
 aim
 of
 this
 review
 is
 to
 evaluate
 the
 burden
 of
 VPDs
 during
MGs.
2.
 Search
 strategy
2.1.
 Search
 strategy
 and
 selection
 criteria
Studies
 were
 identiﬁed
 through
 searching
 electronic
 databases
including;
 PubMed
 and
 Embase
 from
 database
 inception
 to
 April
30,
 2015.
 We  used
 a
 combination
 of
 MeSH
 terms
 and
 text
 words
including:
 ‘mass
 gathering’
 OR
 ‘holiday’
 OR
 ‘sport’
 OR
 ‘festival’
 OR
‘Hajj’
 OR
 ‘FIFA’,
 OR
 ‘pilgrimage’
 AND
 ‘infection’
 OR
 ‘disease
 out-
break’
 OR’
 respiratory
 disease’
 OR
 ‘inﬂuenza’
 OR
 ‘communicable
disease’
 AND
 ‘vaccine’
 OR
 ‘vaccination’
 OR
 ‘immunisation’
 OR
 ‘vac-
cine
 uptake’.
 Google
 Scholar
 and
 pertinent
 websites
 including
 the
online
 Saudi
 Epidemiology
 Bulletin
 were
 also
 searched
 for
 relevant
studies.
 Finally,
 manual
 searches
 were
 performed
 reviewing
 ref-
erence
 lists
 of
 included
 studies
 to
 identify
 additional
 potentially
relevant
 papers.
Inclusion
 criteria
 were
 all
 observational
 studies
 that
 reported
the
 rate
 of
 infectious
 diseases
 preventable
 by
 vaccine
 e.g.,
 inﬂuenza,
meningococcal
 and
 pneumococcal
 diseases,
 pertussis,
 diphtheria,
measles,
 mumps,
 rubella,
 hepatitis
 A
 and
 B,
 tetanus,
 diphtheria
 and
varicella.
 The
 target
 population
 of
 this
 review
 was
 attendees
 at
 MGs
of
 any
 age,
 gender
 and
 country.
 Only
 English
 language
 studies
 were
included.
We
 excluded
 case
 reports,
 case
 series
 and
 studies
 involv-
ing
 healthcare
 workers
 or
 that
 focussed
 on
 health
 services
 or
infrastructure
 during
 MGs.
 Studies
 reporting
 infections
 not
 pre-
ventable
 by
 vaccine
 were
 also
 excluded.
A
 selective
 strategy
 was
 used
 for
 older
 papers
 that
 reported
occurrence
 of
 repeated
 outbreaks
 such
 as
 cholera
 at
 Hajj
 and
 Kubh
Mela
 whereby
 only
 the
 most
 comprehensive
 articles
 were
 included.
Similarly,
 for
 the
 meningococcal
 outbreaks
 at
 Hajj,
 only
 the
 key
outbreak
 reports
 were
 included.
Two
 authors
 (ASA
 and
 MA)
 independently
 reviewed
 the
 titles
and
 abstracts
 to
 identify
 potentially
 relevant
 papers.
 Full
 texts
of
 all
 potentially
 relevant
 papers
 were
 reviewed
 by
 both
 authors
to
 determine
 those
 which
 met
 the
 selection
 criteria.
 The
 ‘pre-
ferred
 reporting
 items
 for
 systematic
 reviews
 and
 meta-analyses
(PRISMA)’
 statement
 was
 used
 to
 guide
 and
 report
 the
 search
methodology
 (Fig.
 1).
2.2.
 Data
 extraction
Two
 authors
 (ASA
 and
 MA)
 independently
 extracted
 the
 data
from
 each
 study
 into
 a
 data
 extraction
 sheet
 which
 was
 divided
 in
sections
 for
 each
 MG  event,
 while
 a
 third
 author
 (HR)
 arbitrated
when
 a
 discrepancy
 occurred.
 The
 following
 data
 were
 abstracted
in
 each
 extraction
 sheet:
 study
 design,
 year
 and
 country
 of
 conduct-
ing
 the
 study,
 sample
 size,
 age
 group,
 diagnostic
 method
 used,
 type
and
 prevalence
 of
 infection,
 vaccination
 rate
 and
 effectiveness
 of
vaccine,
 if
 available.
 Whenever
 possible
 we
 provided
 a
 pooled
 esti-
mate
 of
 the
 burden
 of
 VPDs
 otherwise
 a
 narrative
 synthesis
 was
conducted.

Included
 studies
 reported
 outbreaks/diseases
 among
 partici-
pants
 of
 MGs
 that
 took
 place
 in
 Saudi
 Arabia,
 India,
 USA,
 UK,
 Qatar,
Australia,
 Singapore,
 Slovenia
 and
 Belgium,
 between
 1893
 and
2014.
 Participants
 (i.e.,
 travellers)
 from
 a
 number
 of
 countries
 were
represented
 including,
 Saudi
 Arabia,
 India,
 France,
 Iran,
 Singapore,
UK,
 USA,
 Australia,
 Slovenia,
 Belgium,
 Ghana,
 and
 elsewhere.
 Most
reported
 diseases
 among
 the
 studies
 were
 air
 borne
 infections
including
 inﬂuenza,
 measles,
 meningococcal
 disease
 and
 tubercu-
losis.
3.2.
 Religious
 MGs
3.2.1.
 Hajj
 pilgrimage
Each
 year,
 the
 Hajj
 pilgrimage
 to
 Mecca,
 Saudi
 Arabia
 attracts
about
 2–3
 million
 Muslims
 from
 different
 countries
 around
 the
world
 in
 the
 last
 month
 (12th
 month)
 of
 the
 Arabic
 calendar
 (Dhu
 Al
Hijjah)
 [12]. The
 risk
 of
 infectious
 diseases
 among
 the
 attendees
 of
Hajj
 is
 high.
 Vaccination
 against
 meningococcal
 disease
 is
 manda-
tory
 for
 all,
 and
 yellow
 fever
 and
 polio
 vaccines
 are
 also
 mandatory
for
 pilgrims
 from
 endemic
 countries.
 Additionally
 pilgrims
 are
 rec-
ommended
 to
 remain
 up
 to
 date
 with
 other
 vaccines,
 including
seasonal
 inﬂuenza
 vaccine
 [17].
 However,
 vaccine
 uptake
 among
pilgrims
 is
 highly
 variable
 [18].
We
 have
 identiﬁed
 26
 key
 studies
 reporting
 the
 disease
 burden
at
 Hajj,
 of
 these
 15
 reported
 transmission
 of
 inﬂuenza,
 occur-
rence
 of
 other
 infections
 such
 as
 meningococcal
 and
 pneumococcal
diseases,
 tuberculosis,
 and
 pertussis
 were
 reported,
 and
 from
 his-
torical
 reports,
 one
 key
 publication
 on
 cholera
 has
 been
 included.
3.2.1.1.
 Cholera.
 Repeated
 outbreaks
 of
 cholera
 have
 occurred
 at
Hajj
 over
 the
 centuries
 [19–21]. Omar
 listed
 the
 outbreaks
 of
cholera
 occurring
 between
 1877
 and
 1912;
 the
 number
 of
 fatalities
in
 those
 outbreaks
 ranged
 from
 306
 to
 32,994
 [22]. The
 largest
cholera
 outbreak
 happened
 in
 1893,
 with
 the
 fatality
 rate
 reach-
ing
 up
 to
 1000
 cases
 per
 day
 [23]. The
 Hajj
 assembly
 in
 1893
commenced
 on
 23rd
 of
 June,
 and
 cholera
 was
 declared
 amongst
returned
 pilgrims
 in
 Jeddah
 on
 June
 29th;
 by
 July
 13th
 over
 10,000
fatalities
 were
 reported
 from
 Mecca
 and
 Jeddah
 [24]. Consequently,
the
 Hajj
 season
 ended
 with
 a
 total
 death
 toll
 of
 32,994
 [22].
 Sev-
eral
 control
 measures,
 including
 quarantine
 and
 vaccination,
 were
instituted,
 and
 although
 the
 disease
 was
 ultimately
 brought
 under
control,
 the
 vaccination
 programme
 confronted
 resistance
 due
 to
cultural,
 religious
 and
 political
 conﬂicts
 [19]. The
 last
 known
 out-
break
 of
 cholera
 occurred
 in
 1989
 (Table
 2)
 [12].
3.2.1.2.
 Meningococcal
 disease.
 Two
 major
 outbreaks
 of
 meningo-
coccal
 
disease
 
are
 
well
 
known:
 
the
 
ﬁrst
 
was
 
caused
 
by
meningococcal
 serogroup
 A
 in
 1987
 which
 affected
 about
 2000
 pil-
grims
 in
 various
 countries,
 and
 the
 next
 was
 caused
 by
 serogroup
W135
 in
 2000–2001
 that
 affected
 collectively
 around
 2400
 pilgrims
across
 the
 globe
 [25].
 Extensive
 molecular
 investigation
 revealed
that
 the
 1987
 outbreak
 originated
 in
 Nepal
 in
 1983–1984,
 spread
to
 Delhi
 in
 1985
 and
 was
 brought
 to
 Mecca
 by
 Hajj
 pilgrims
 in
1987
 [26], whence
 it
 spread
 to
 neighbouring
 countries,
 and
 reached
Europe,
 notably
 UK
 [27,28]. Within
 4
 weeks
 of
 Hajj,
 15
 cases
 were
recorded
 in
 Qatar,
 six
 of
 them
 were
 returned
 pilgrims
 and
 the
rest
 were
 secondary
 cases
 [28]. In
 the
 UK
 alone
 there
 were
 18
cases
 of
 meningococcal
 A
 disease
 among
 returned
 pilgrims
 and
 15
additional
 cases
 among
 the
 contacts
 [27].
 Subsequently,
 the
 Saudi
Arabian
 health
 authority
 mandated
 bivalent
 A
 and
 C
 vaccine
 for
 all
pilgrims
 [25].
The
 2000–2001
 outbreaks
 have
 been
 separately
 described
 in
a
 few
 key
 reviews
 [12,25]. Memish
 and
 colleagues
 have
 pro-
vided
 surveillance
 data
 on
 meningococcal
 disease
 (1995–2011)

established

 to

 or

inﬂuenza
 and
 their

 (Table
 2)
 [9].

3.4.
 Entertainment
 MGs
3.4.1.
 Music
 concert
 ‘Jam
 bands’
‘Jam
 bands’
 is

 music
 festival
 that
 involves
 camping
on
 established
 or
 unplanned
 campgrounds
 where
 the
 sanitary
 con-
dition
 is
 below
 optimum
 standard.
 The
 bands
 performing
 at
 these
concerts
 and
 festivals
 attract
 between
 1200
 and
 82,000
 fans
 that
travel
 from
 one

 to

 sometimes
 over

 months
often

 on

 from

 Concerts
 may
 go
 on
for
 days.
 An

 of

 A
 occurred

of

 bands”

 in

 in

 A
 total
 of

cases
 with

 age
 of

 (range
 17–44
 years)
 were

among

 from

 US

 [65].
 Following

investigation,

 and

 were

 in
an

 to
 curb
 the
 outbreak.

 Rock
 Werchter

Rock

 of

 It

 as

 of

biggest

 in

 It

80,000

 of

 mostly
 in

 of
July
 or
 at

 of

 In

 hosted
 the

in

 ﬁrst

 of

 with

 69,000

 at

 of
pandemic
 inﬂuenza.
 Gutiérrez
 et
 al.
 recorded
 12

 cases
 of
inﬂuenza
 A(H1N1)pdm09
 out
 of
 30
 (40%)
 young

 with
ILI
 symptoms
 (Table
 2) [66].
 Two

from

 and

 of

linked

Every

 of

 It

 in

 We  found

 reporting

 to
a

 that

 and
 9

to

 A

 of

 to

were

 of

 at

the

 or

 At
 least
 ﬁve
unvaccinated

 were

 (Table
 2)
 [67].

 Disney
 Theme

Disney
 Parks
 are
 among
 the
 biggest
 theme
 parks
 in
 the
 world,
attracting
 around
 16
 million
 guests
 of

 from

 round

 Recently
 a

 in

 Parks
 [68]. At
 the

 of

public

 of

Disneyland
 Park,
 and

 California
 Adventure

 Individ-
uals
 of

 Of
34

4.
 Discussion
The
 review
 canvases
 the
 many
 infectious
 risks
 associated
 with
MGs
 and
 demonstrates
 that
 a
 variety
 of
 VPDs
 occur
 at
 these
 large
public
 events.
 The
 Hajj
 pilgrimage
 is
 the
 most
 studied
 MG,
 and
currently
 respiratory
 tract
 infections
 are
 the
 commonest
 health
hazards
 among
 the
 attendees
 of
 most
 MGs.
Inﬂuenza,
 meningococcal
 disease
 and
 measles
 are
 the
 most
commonly
 identiﬁed
 infectious
 diseases.
 This
 is
 likely
 because
 the
uniquely
 high
 ‘contact
 rates’
 in
 a
 crowded
 milieu
 allow
 the
 air-
borne
 microbes
 to
 transmit
 easily
 [69], and
 their
 relatively
 shorter
incubation
 period
 allows
 their
 earlier
 detection.
Inﬂuenza
 has
 been
 reported
 from
 both
 sport
 (Winter
 Olympics,
Asian
 Youth
 Games)
 and
 religious
 (Hajj,
 WYD)
 MGs.
 Although
travellers
 (e.g.,
 Hajj
 pilgrims)
 are
 encouraged
 to
 obtain
 inﬂuenza
vaccine
 before
 they
 travel
 to
 the
 event,
 the
 uptake
 is
 variable,
 but
encouragingly
 increasing
 in
 recent
 years
 [70]. Consequently,
 the
rate
 of
 ILI
 is
 decreasing,
 and
 a
 meta-analysis
 of
 observational
 studies
demonstrated
 a
 signiﬁcant
 effect
 of
 vaccination
 on
 laboratory-
proven
 inﬂuenza
 although
 the
 overall
 effect
 of
 the
 vaccine
 on
disease
 is
 yet
 to
 be
 seen
 and
 controlled
 studies
 exploring
 the
 efﬁ-
cacy
 of
 inﬂuenza
 vaccine
 among
 attendees
 of
 MGs
 are
 not
 available
[18,71].
Although
 outbreaks
 of
 meningococcal
 disease
 have
 been
 con-
sidered
 typical
 of
 Hajj
 [25]; they
 have
 occurred
 elsewhere
 e.g.,
 an
outbreak
 at
 the
 2004
 European
 Football
 Tournament
 (EURO
 2004)
[72]. Mandatory
 meningococcal
 vaccination
 of
 pilgrims
 and
 resi-
dents
 living
 around
 pilgrimage
 sites
 reduced
 the
 incidence
 of
 the
invasive
 disease
 not
 only
 among
 travellers
 but
 also
 among
 the
native
 population
 [73]; however
 the
 polysaccharide
 vaccine,
 the
most
 widely
 used
 vaccine
 [74], is
 unlikely
 to
 impact
 pharyngeal
carriage
 of
 meningococci
 and
 thus
 would
 not
 prevent
 transmis-
sion
 of
 pathogenic
 strains
 to
 pilgrims’
 contacts
 [75]. A
 conjugate
quadrivalent
 vaccine
 is
 more
 likely
 to
 prevent
 acquisition
 and
perhaps
 also
 clears
 throat
 carriage
 and
 thus
 provide
 community
protection
 [76]. In
 view
 of
 the
 changing
 epidemiology
 of
 the
 dis-
ease
 in
 countries
 that
 send
 pilgrims
 to
 Hajj,
 some
 experts
 have
stressed
 the
 importance
 of
 meningococcal
 B
 vaccine
 for
 Hajj
 pil-
grims
 [77].
Outbreaks
 of
 measles
 linked
 to
 both
 sport
 and
 entertainment
events
 have
 been
 reported
 [63,67,68]. In
 regions
 with
 interrupted
endemic
 measles
 transmission,
 imported
 and
 import-related
 cases
account
 for
 virtually
 all
 measles
 in
 the
 area,
 but
 in
 regions
 with
high
 vaccine
 uptake
 (≥95%)
 the
 transmission
 of
 the
 disease
 was
very
 limited
 [78].
 Consequently,
 almost
 all
 measles
 outbreaks
 at
MGs
 have
 occurred
 among
 unvaccinated
 or
 incompletely
 vacci-
nated
 individuals.
 We  are
 not
 aware
 of
 any
 outbreak
 of
 measles
in
 association
 with
 Hajj
 pilgrimage
 although
 an
 outbreak
 among
attendees
 of
 a
 catholic
 festival
 in
 Taizé,
 France
 is
 reported
 [62].
Relatively
 older
 age
 of
 Hajj
 pilgrims
 and
 a
 high
 level
 of
 immunity
among
 the
 attendees
 could
 be
 the
 possible
 explanation.
 Outbreak
of
 mumps
 resulting
 from
 incomplete
 protection
 following
 a
 single
dose
 of
 MMR
 vaccine
 has
 been
 reported
 at
 MG  setting
 [61]. Simi-
larly,
 an
 outbreak
 of
 rubella
 occurred
 among
 unvaccinated
 young
male
 cadets
 deployed
 at
 Hajj
 in
 1995,
 but
 pilgrims
 were
 not
 affected
[79].
Large
 cholera
 outbreaks
 are
 no
 more
 reported
 at
 MGs
 [20];
however
 other
 water
 and
 foodborne
 diseases
 preventable
 by
 vac-
cine
 are
 occasionally
 reported.
 Occurrence
 of
 hepatitis
 A
 following
‘Jam
 bands’
 concerts
 in
 the
 USA
 is
 one
 such
 example.
 Raﬁq
 and
colleagues
 discussed
 that
 although
 hepatitis
 A
 has
 not
 yet
 been
reported
 from
 Hajj,
 many
 pilgrims
 engage
 in
 high
 risk
 behaviours
such
 as
 buying
 foods
 from
 unregulated
 vendors,
 communal
 use
 of

toilets,
 avoidance
 of

 soap
 and

 iced
 water
 [65].
Similarly,
 the
 risk
 of
 food
 and
 waterborne
 diseases,
 both
 vaccine-
preventable,
 and
 non-preventable
 infections,
 is
 high.
 For
 instance,
recent
 qualitative
 observation
 and
 epidemiological
 surveillance
 at
Kumbh
 Mela
 suggest
 that
 the
 level
 of
 water
 sanitation
 and
 hygiene
of
 bathing
 water
 of
 Kumbh
 fair
 is
 substandard
 [80]. Similarly,
 food
borne
 outbreaks
 have
 been
 reported
 at
 other
 mass
 events
 such
 as
FIFA
 World
 Cup
 2006
 in
 Germany,
 where
 an
 outbreak
 of
 norovirus
affected
 over
 60
 attendees
 [81].
VPDs
 that
 transmit
 through
 the
 sexual
 or
 parenteral
 route
 such
as
 hepatitis
 B
 and
 human
 papilloma
 virus
 are
 not
 widely
 reported.
However
 there
 is
 a
 considerable
 risk
 of
 spread
 of
 these
 infec-
tions
 at
 MGs.
 For
 instance,
 the
 risk
 of
 transmission
 of
 hepatitis
 B
among
 athletes
 of
 contact
 sports
 can
 be
 high
 [82,83]. Similarly,
pilgrims
 at
 Hajj
 and
 Kumbh
 Mela
 engage
 in
 other
 activities
 (e.g.,
communal
 use
 of
 razors
 for
 head
 shaving)
 that
 may
 increase
 the
risk
 of
 parenteral
 hepatitis
 [57]. Due
 to
 the
 prolonged
 incubation
period
 of
 these
 infections,
 systematic
 case
 ﬁnding
 is
 challeng-
ing.
Transmission
 of
 drug
 resistant
 pathogens
 such
 as
 S.
 pneumo-
niae
 is
 a
 growing
 risk
 at
 MGs
 [84]. It
 is
 postulated
 that
 about
 one
third
 of
 pneumococcal
 isolates
 are
 resistant
 to
 penicillin
 and
 one
tenth
 resistant
 to
 third
 generation
 cephalosporins
 [51,85].
 Circu-
lation
 of
 highly
 penicillin
 resistant
 S.
 pneumoniae
 in
 Spain
 at
 the
time
 of
 Barcelona
 Olympics
 in
 1992
 was
 of
 concern
 [86].
 Conjugate
pneumococcal
 vaccines
 can
 play
 a
 role
 in
 preventing
 transmission
of
 antibiotic
 resistant
 pneumococci
 [87].
The
 type
 of
 MGs
 and
 the
 activities
 of
 the
 participants
 can
inﬂuence
 the
 epidemiology
 of
 VPDs
 at
 MGs.
 For
 instance,
 respi-
ratory
 infections
 including
 pneumonia
 (such
 as
 inﬂuenza
 or
pneumococcal
 pneumonia)
 are
 the
 common
 health
 hazards
 among
Hajj
 pilgrims,
 half
 of
 whom
 are
 older
 than
 50
 years
 of
 age
 [88,89].
 On
the
 other
 hand,
 measles
 and
 mumps
 are
 common
 among
 attendees
of
 sport
 and
 fun
 events
 which
 are
 primarily
 attended
 by
 the
 young
[18,51]. Inﬂuenza,
 on
 the
 other
 hand,
 is
 common
 in
 both
 religious
and
 sport
 events
 [6].
 Local
 epidemiology
 of
 the
 host
 country
 also
inﬂuences
 the
 disease
 pattern.
 For
 instance,
 outbreaks
 of
 cholera
at
 Kumbh
 Mela
 festivals
 in
 the
 Ganges
 basin
 are
 unsurprising
 since
historically
 the
 Ganges
 is
 known
 to
 be
 a
 source
 of
 explosive
 out-
breaks
 of
 cholera
 [10,90]. Similarly,
 the
 pharyngeal
 carriage
 rate
 of
meningococci
 in
 the
 most
 crowded
 part
 of
 Mecca
 was
 as
 high
 as
86%
 during
 the
 interim
 period
 between
 the
 two
 major
 outbreaks
 of
meningococcal
 disease
 at
 Hajj
 [91].
From
 the
 examples
 of
 intercontinental
 outbreaks
 of
 meningo-
coccal
 disease,
 and
 globalisation
 of
 respiratory
 pathogens
 from
 Hajj
[2,26], outbreaks
 of
 cholera
 from
 Kumbh
 Mela
 and
 Hajj
 [60], and
international
 spread
 of
 measles
 from
 MGs
 [78], it
 is
 apparent
 that
spread
 of
 infectious
 disease
 can
 have
 far
 reaching
 ramiﬁcations.
Vaccination
 can
 play
 a
 key
 role
 in
 the
 prevention
 of
 VPDs.
 Pre-
departure
 vaccination
 of
 a
 cohort
 of
 Egyptian
 pilgrims
 brought
the
 prevalence
 of
 pandemic
 inﬂuenza
 A(H1N1)pdm09
 to
 nil
 dur-
ing
 the
 Hajj
 in
 October
 2009
 [92]. Furthermore,
 good
 planning
 and
effective
 surveillance
 systems
 are
 other
 key
 components
 of
 control-
ling
 infectious
 disease
 at
 MGs.
 For
 example,
 by
 planning
 well
 and
strengthening
 public
 health
 services
 the
 London
 2012
 Olympics
proceeded
 without
 major
 incident,
 similarly
 Hajj
 and
 Asian
 Youth
Games
 were
 organised
 during
 2009
 pandemic
 inﬂuenza
 with
 no
discernable
 upsurge
 of
 the
 disease
 [8,93].
In
 recent
 years,
 experts
 have
 recommended
 vaccines
 for
 travel-
lers
 who
 intended
 to
 attend
 mass
 gathering
 events.
 For
 instance,

 in
 South
 Africa,
 were
 recommended
 to
 receive
vaccinations
 against
 hepatitis
 A
 and
 B,
 inﬂuenza,
 typhoid,
 and
MMR
 [96]. Moreover,
 because
 of
 the
 higher
 risk
 of
 meningococ-
cal
 disease
 in
 Africa,
 some
 experts
 additionally
 recommended
 the
quadrivalent
 meningococcal
 vaccine
 for
 travellers
 to
 South
 Africa
[97]. Travellers
 to
 the
 FIFA
 2014,
 Brazil,
 were
 advised
 to
 remain
up-to-date
 with
 routine
 vaccines,
 including
 hepatitis
 A,
 inﬂuenza,
MMR,
 tetanus,
 diphtheria,
 pertussis,
 pneumococcal
 and
 varicella
[98]. Further
 recommendation
 has
 also
 been
 issued
 for
 travellers
to
 Brazil
 to
 participate
 in
 the
 2016
 Olympic
 Games;
 the
 spectators
have
 been
 particularly
 advised
 to
 receive
 inﬂuenza
 and
 measles
vaccines
 [99].
Despite
 the
 recognised
 beneﬁts
 of
 vaccination,
 uptake
 among
attendees
 of
 MGs
 is
 variable
 and
 often
 suboptimal.
 For
 instance,
Memish
 et
 al.
 demonstrated
 that
 despite
 compulsory
 requirement,
about
 7%
 pilgrims
 did
 not
 receive
 appropriate
 meningococcal
 vac-
cine
 [74], and
 concerns
 were
 raised
 about
 the
 issuing
 of
 false
meningococcal
 certiﬁcates
 to
 some
 South
 African
 pilgrims
 a
 decade
ago
 [100]. Studies
 found
 that
 lack
 of
 knowledge
 about
 vaccine
 was
an
 important
 barrier
 for
 vaccination
 [43,101]. Education
 of
 health
professionals
 and
 involvement
 of
 tour
 group
 leaders
 might
 improve
vaccination
 coverage
 [102].
To
 our
 knowledge,
 this
 is
 the
 ﬁrst
 review
 that
 has
 assessed
 the
burden
 of
 VPDs
 at
 MGs.
 VPDs
 can
 occur
 at
 diverse
 types
 of
 events
including
 religious,
 sports
 and
 entertainment
 events.
 An
 impor-
tant
 limitation
 is
 that
 studies
 reporting
 syndromic
 infections
 were
excluded
 from
 our
 review
 and
 most
 studies
 reported
 only
 syn-
dromic
 data.
 Moreover,
 in
 this
 review
 we
 did
 not
 evaluate
 the
effectiveness
 of
 vaccines.
 The
 limited
 available
 data
 suggest
 that
the
 vaccines
 are
 effective
 against
 meningococcal
 disease,
 inﬂuenza
and
 cholera
 [18,19,59,71,73]. Studies
 are
 needed
 to
 quantify
 the
effects
 of
 vaccines
 more
 precisely.
5.
 Conclusion
VPDs
 have
 been
 reported
 at
 many
 MGs.
 Infections
 not
 only
 affect
the
 participants
 of
 MGs
 but
 also
 their
 contacts.
 Despite
 recommen-
dations
 issued
 by
 experts,
 event
 organisers
 or
 international
 public
health
 bodies
 the
 uptake
 of
 vaccines
 is
 variable
 and
 generally
 low,
and
 there
 is
 a
 dearth
 of
 data
 on
 the
 effectiveness
 of
 vaccines
 at
 MGs.
Research
 should
 be
 inspired
 to
 address
 those
 gaps.
